title: Mired In The Labor-Market Malaise
gist: Gavekal Dragonomics' Ernan Cui argues China's labor market never recovered from the pandemic: 2025 data show the agricultural workforce share rose for only the second time in decades, construction employment is in slow-motion collapse, and services hiring is cooling, with export-driven manufacturing the sole bright spot, insufficient to tighten the market. Paywalled teaser.

insights.claim: China's labor market never truly recovered after the pandemic, and 2025 data show the share of the workforce in agriculture rose for only the second time in decades as fewer urban job opportunities pushed people back into farming.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: A rare reversal of urbanization signals deep labor slack; bearish for Chinese household income, consumption and consumer-exposed equities.
insights.quote: The share of the workforce engaged in agriculture rose for only the second time in decades, as fewer urban job opportunities pushed more people into farming.
insights.locator: China’s labor market never truly recovered

insights.claim: Employment in China's construction sector is in a slow-motion collapse and labor demand in services is cooling, with export-driven manufacturing the only sector where hiring and wages are picking up.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Confirms property/construction drag and service softness; manufacturing strength is export-dependent, leaving the recovery vulnerable to trade shocks.
insights.quote: Employment in construction is in a slow-motion collapse, and labor demand in services is cooling. The sole bright spot is the manufacturing sector
insights.locator: China’s labor market never truly recovered

insights.claim: Gavekal expects continued Chinese labor-market slack to keep sapping household income and spending growth in coming months, with manufacturing alone insufficient to tighten the market.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Implies weak Chinese consumption persists; supports caution on China consumer demand and reflation hopes near-term.
insights.quote: labor-market slack will continue to sap household income and spending growth in the coming months.
insights.locator: More recent jobs data provide little reason
